00002The special counsel dropped both efforts after Trump won in November, in light of a Justice Department policy against prosecuting a sitting president.L
In New York. Justice Elen
Gesmer held a hearing Tuesday on whether Trump's hushmoney sentencing, set for Friday, could go forward.
concluded, she issued a onesentence order rejecting Trump's request to stop the proceedings on the grounds of presidential immunity.
for the notion that presidential immunity extends to presidential elects?" Gesmer asked Trump lawyer Todd Blanche
"There has never been a case like this before, so no,”Blanche replied.
Steven Wu, a lawyer for the Manhattan district attorney's office, called Trump's arguments baseless.
"The theory of presidentelect immunity is inconsistent with the idea that there is one president at a time," he told the judge.
The proceedings come as courts and prosecutors are
scrambling to wrap up their unprecedented cases against Trump before Inauguration Day.

to remove Smith as special counsel. Among the letter's signatories were lawyers Trump has picked for top Justice Department posts, including Blanche, his choice for deputy attorney general.

"Releasing Smith's report is obviously not in the public interest—particularly in light of President Trump's commanding victory in the election and the sensitive nature of the ongoing transition process,"Trump's lawyers wrote.

[Smith's team, in a court filing Tuesday, said it was working to finalize a two-vol ume report and that, if Gar land eled wouldn't happen before Fri day. In a court filing later or Tuesday, Smith said his office would file a more detailed re sponse in the 11th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals.
司a jury last year convicted Trump of 34 felonies for cov ering up hush money paid to a porn star on the eve of the 2016 election.
In a surprise rulinglast week, Justice Juan Merchan who presided over the trial said he would sentence Trum before the start of his second term, but made clear that jai time wasn't a possibility.
Merchan on Monday de clined to postpone the sen tencing,prompting Trump's lawyers to ask a state appeals court to intervene.
It wasn't immediately clear what legal options Trump may have remaining to try to h off Friday's proceedings.

Trump has accused Panama of charging U.S. shipping and naval vessels high fees for passage through the canal.

'People really don't even know if Denmark has any legal right to it,' Trump said.

Trunp aiso said his administration would change the name of the Gulf of Mexico to the Gulf of America."What a beautiful name, and it's appropriate," he said.

添司添conference, Trump's eldest son, Donald Trump Jr., arrived in Greenland-in a Trumpbranded plane. The elder Trump wrote in a social-media post, "This is a deal that must happen.MAGA.MAKE GREENLAND GREAT AGAIN!"

The president-elect called into a lunchtime gathering of his son and others in Greenland, saying over a speaker phone, "It's a very special place. It needs security for itself, but it also needs security very much for the world."
Donald Trump Jr. arrived in Nuuk, Greenland, on Tuesday ahead of his father's news conference

The younger Trump arrived on Tuesday in Nuuk, Greenland's capital, with an entourage that included conservative activist Charlie Kirk; James Blair, political director of the 2024 campaign and incoming senior White House adviser;and Sergio Gor, who will oversee the White House Office of Presidential Personnel.
Trump's national-security team has had preliminary dis
cussions about a process by which Trump would direct the State Department to undertake negotiations with the governments of Denmark and Greenland, according to people close to the
transition. That could potentially happen through a Compacts of Free Association agreement—a financial assistance pact similar to what the U.S. currently has with several Pacific Island nations.
Trump's advisers hope to launch potential negotiations once the president-elect takes office, the people said.
But the Trump administration is likely to pursue a negotiation that is more nuanced
than a real-estate purchase or sweeping annexation, according to the people close to the transition.
During his first term,Trump expressed varying levels of interest in purchasing Greenland after hearing from his aides about its abundant resources and geopolitical importance.
When The Wall Street Journal first reported of Trump's interest in the purchase,Greenland's Ministry of Foreign Affairs said that the island wasn't on the market.
Trump's Tuesday news conference touched on several other topics. On the negotiations to release hostages

seized in Israel by Hamas militants in Gaza, Trump said, "If they're not back by the time I get into office, all hell will break out in the Middle East,and it will not be good for Hamas, and it will not be good, frankly, for anyone."Trump has appointed Steve Witkoff, a real-estate executive and close ally, as his envoy to the Middle East. On Wednesday, Witkoff is expected to join talks in Doha,Qatar, for a cease-fire in Gaza in exchange for the releases of hostages held by Hamas.
司2021, attack on the U.S. Capitol, the president-elect said he is looking at potential pardons

for participants charged in the riot, including those charged with violent offenses. "People that were doing some bad things weren't prosecuted and people that didn't even walk into the building are in jail right now," he said. "So we'll be looking at the whole thing, but I'll be making major pardons."

The president-elect said he is open to pushing his legisla tive agenda through Congress with either a single large bil or two separate bills, weighing in on an issue that has ben point of debate for lawmakers on Capitol Hill. Trump is ex pected to visit Capitol Hill or Wednesday.
Debt-Ceiling Battle  This Time Around  Offers New Twists
YRICHRDUBIN
WASHINGTON-Here we go gain.
ng the debt limit, triggering familiar cycle of brinkmannip, bipartisannegotiation nd possible cii.Th o,owever, has a few twists.

Republicans have the power raise the debt ceilingby nemselves, and they willtry do that in their tax, border,nergy and spending-cuts leglation that would wrap many President-electDonald rump's priorities into one bill."With all that we're doing 0restorefiscal healthand rosperity, we ought to put he debt ceiling in there as yell,becausethosearethe scal reforms that I think hould be required to do it,"aid Rep.Jodey Arrington (R.,exas), chairman of the House udget Committee.“And rump's committed to it."

添司If Republicans' efforts colpse, they will need to turn to emocrats to avert a potential overnment defaultaround e middle of the year, possiy setting the stage for more eadline drama. While many emocrats have made clear ney won't sign off on a GOP lan that kicks the debt ceilng to the next Congress or ext president, they have sig
naled support for effectively getting rid of the debt-ceilling threat altogether.

a long-term or permanent debtceiling increase—which fell short among Republicans—could become the basis for an agreement with Democrats,said Rep. Brendan Boyle (D.,Pa.). Boyle said he is open to working with Trump on a bill that would permanently alter the debt limit, aiming to prevent Republicans from wielding it against a future Democratic president like they did in 2011 and 2023.

公司添est and for the good of the country, this is a great opportunity for Democrats to once and forall permanently resolve this issue so it can never again be used as a weapon,"said Boyle, the top Democrat on the Budget Committe,who has introduced legislation that would let the administration raise the debt limit unless Congress objects with vetoproof majorities.
Trump, he said, "may actually be someone that Democrats can work with in order to achieve our long-term goals."
House Speaker Mike Johnson (R., La.) has said he wants to include a debt-limit increase in the "one big beautiful bill" that Republicans will
U.S. debt held by the publlic $30 trillion
Source: Treasury Department

try to move through Congress using a procedure known as budget reconciliation, which averts the 60-vote Senate filibuster threshold. Republicans have a narrow 219-215 majority in the House, which is expected to temporarily shrink to 217-215 when two members  join the administration.

Johnson reiterated Tuesday that Republicans want to handle the debt ceiling without relying on Democrats, but he acknowledged there was "a broad range of opinion" about the borrowing limit among GOP colleagues.

For decades, Congress periodically raised the limit, often tying it to budget agreements with few threats of a breach.Modern debt-limit politics started in 201l1, when the Republican House insisted that any debt-ceiling increase be paired with spending cuts.
"They've certainly become more frequent, more often down to the wire and more political in nature," said Shai
Akabas, who tracks debt-limit debates for the Bipartisan Policy Center. "It has not proved to be an effective tool to force Congress to enact debt-reduction policies."
That 20ll negotiation with then-President Barack Obama brought the U.S. to the ede of default and led to a downgraded credit rating. House
Republicans repeated that strategy with President Biden in 2023. Both times, Republicans won concessions and Democrats insisted they would never again negotiate on the debt ceiling.
That 2023 deal suspended the limit until this month. Potential missed payments won't happen until later because the
Treasury Department can use tax revenue and extraordinary measures—moving money around government accountsto stave off the so-called date for a few more months.

Trump, seeingthe ceiling on the horizon, asked Con gress to include a debt-limi House Republicans voted for two-year debt-limit suspen sion, even without spendin increase in an end-of-yea spending bill in late 2024,tion. The vast majority o cuts attached.month before his inaugura
WARRINOTON
Rep. Jodey Arrington (R., Texas) said Republicans should raise the debt limit as part of their broader fiscal legislation.

But enough House Republi cans objected—joined by Dem ocrats—to prevent that bil from passing, and the ultimate spending bill lacked a debt limit increase. Trump askec Congress again to raise ol eliminate the debt ceiling be fore he takes office Jan. 20but lawmakers haven't acted
"All I want to see is no de fault because nobody knows what would happen if there is a default," Trump said Tues day. "It could be 199 or i could be nothing.“

Ea litically diicult, because law makers don't like to be seen as enabling higher debt and defi cits. The cuts demanded by some Republicans along witl a debt-limit increase migh prove unacceptable to othe Republicans.

"When your House majorit is one seat, it's just going t be really, really difficult to ge a debt limit over the finisl line simply with Republicar votes," said Paul Winfree, wh was a Trump aide in the firs term and is now president o the Economic Policy Innova
How the Debt Limit Works

The debt limit is a weird creature. Congress created the limit before World War Il to make debt management smoother while setting a cap on overall red ink to keep the legislature involved. But now the debt limit is a source of friction;failing to raise it threatens a global financial crisis.
Raising or freezing the debt limit doesn't change the U.S.'s fiscal trajectory.
Instead, raising the limit just lets the Treasury Department borrow money to implement Congress's prior tax and spending decisions.

司crease, the U.S. would be unable to pay all its bills, at a minimum risking missed payments to federal workers or Medicare providers and potentially triggering damaging disruptions to the Treasury market.
